text: An experimental determination has been made of the heat transfer coefficients for molten sodium hydroxide flowing in turbulent forced convection through a tube of circular cross section and a length to diameter ratio of 200.
Heat transfer coefficients in the region of fully developed turbulent flow are reported for the Reynolds modulus range of 6000-12000 and the temperature range of $700 - 900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The equation, $\mathrm{Nu} / \mathrm{Pr}^{0.4} = 0.021\mathrm{Re}^{0.8}$ , was found to correlate the data for (L/D) values above 100.
Description of Apparatus 7
Thermal Entrance Region 29
This fluid freezes at $604^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and possesses temperature stability to at least $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

From this aspect nickel was also satisfactory since its electrical conductivity was such as to allow $95\%$ of the heat generation to occur in the tube wall for a reasonably sized experimental system.
The experimental system designed to measure the heat transfer coefficient is illustrated schematically in Figure 1.
Figures 2, 3, and 4 present several views of the apparatus.
The entire system was well-lagged and was maintained at a temperature of $650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
It was possible to read the weight of fluid entering or leaving the weigh tank to within 1/2 pound.
On the basis of these considerations a test section was designed of nickel tubing, $3/16''$ O.D. x 0.035" wall thickness, 24 inches long.
With this test section a flow at a Reynolds modulus of $10^4$ could be obtained with a pressure drop of 35 psi.
The heat generated in the tube wall accounted for $95\%$ of the total heat generation.
A total of 220 pounds of sodium hydroxide was put into the system.
The power was supplied to the test section by a transformer capable of delivering a maximum of 360 amperes at 7.5 volts.
The accuracy of this meter is claimed as $3 / 4\%$ .
Temperatures for control purposes were measured by thermocouples of 28 gage iron and constantan wires located along the tubing and in thermowells in the tanks.
The voltage was measured at the 10 locations along the test section indicated in Figure 5.
The voltage taps were of 28 gage bare copper wire, arc-welded to the tube.
These were then coated with #7 Sauereisen cement to reduce oxidation of the copper at the operating temperature of the system.
The voltage was measured by a multiple-range Ballantine electronic voltmeter, for which an accuracy of $2\%$ is claimed.
A mixing pot is shown in Figure 4.

An analysis of the error due to conduction losses by the thermowell at an average fluid temperature of $950^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ shows the correction to the observed thermocouple reading to be negligibly small.

The current through these heaters was adjusted to maintain a temperature of approximately $650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in the mixing pot so as to prevent freezing of the sodium hydroxide and reduce heat loss from the fluid.
The outside tube wall temperature is shown in Figure 6 as a function of the distance along the test section for various power levels.
Figure 7 shows the system heat loss as a function of $\Delta t$ , where $\Delta t$ is the average outside tube wall temperature, $t_{W,ave}$ , minus the temperature of the system environment, $t_e$ .
The average outside tube wall temperature was obtained by graphical integration of the curves of Figure 6.
Bead thermocouples of 36 gage chromel and alumel wire were calibrated at the melting points of lead and zinc.
The results of these calibrations are indicated in Figure 8.
A maximum correction of $5^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was observed.
Prior to operation, the entire system - sump and weigh tanks and the test unit - was heated to an average temperature of $650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
During a sequence of runs this temperature would drift up to about $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ due to the heat put into the fluid by the test section.
Runs lasted from 20 to 40 minutes depending on the fluid flow rate.
This is indicated in Figure 10 which gives the readings
The tube wall thermocouples were read every 3 to 5 minutes during a run.

where E and I are the voltage and current at the test section, c is the conversion factor, 3.413 Btu/hr-watt, and V is the volume of metal in the tube wall of the test section.
This is borne out by Figure 5 which shows the voltage impressed on the test section as a function of distance along the tube for a typical run.

However, since the heat loss through the power flanges was found to be low, less than $2\%$ of the total heat input, the error introduced by assuming a straight line between $t_{m,i}$ and $t_{m,o}$ is small.

A maximum deviation between these two of $9\%$ was observed.

text: The local coefficient of heat transfer is shown in Figure 13 as a function of the ratio, $(\mathrm{L} / \mathrm{D})$ , for a typical experimental run.
Figure 14 gives the variation of the thermal entrance length, $(\mathrm{L} / \mathrm{D})_{\mathrm{e}}$ , with the Peclet modulus (Re·Pr).

In the present investigation $(\mathrm{L} / \mathrm{D})_{\mathrm{e}}$ was taken as the $(\mathrm{L} / \mathrm{D})$ at which the local conductance had dropped to a value of 1.1 of the established value.

Poppendiek<sup>10</sup> reviews the thermal entrance length data for an air system (Humble, Lowdermilk and Desmon<sup>8</sup> measurements) and for a mercury system (English and Barrett<sup>4</sup> measurements).

Based on the thermal entrance length criterion used in the present report, he found that for air, $(\mathrm{L} / \mathrm{D})_{\mathrm{e}} = 15$ or greater, and for mercury, $(\mathrm{L} / \mathrm{D})_{\mathrm{e}} = 5$ or greater.
